title: The Global Shortage of Tenor Singers: A Voice Made, Not Found
content:
When Andrea Bocelli sang at the recent Olympic opening ceremony in Milan, his thrilling high notes highlighted the unique power of a professional tenor. However, in the ordinary world of amateur and professional choirs, tenors are becoming increasingly scarce. Although thousands of choir groups exist globally, many are painfully short of the voices needed for the crucial tenor section. This shortage has left many conductors desperately begging for more tenors to balance their ensembles. The exact reasons for this decline remain unclear, though some suspect changes in average vocal ranges or physical challenges.

The consequences of this missing voice part are plain to hear in modern choral performances. Much of the classical choral repertoire has become unsingable for choirs that lack a strong tenor section. In desperation, some amateur groups have tried offering lower membership dues or even paying semi-professional singers to temporarily boost their sound. Other choirs have simply plumped for music that completely omits the tenor line, treating all men as mid-range baritones. Unfortunately, this creates a downward spiral where the remaining tenors slowly fade away due to a lack of suitable music.

To combat this trend, musical experts suggest that schools should make singing a higher priority, especially for teenagers whose voices are changing. Many boys stop singing after their voices break because they struggle to transition from singing melodies to handling complex harmonies. Additionally, choirs that can afford it are encouraged to consult professional voice coaches. These coaches might discover that some men hiding in the bass section, or women sitting with the altos, actually possess the vocal range to sing tenor. Ultimately, tenor voices are like gold; they often need to be carefully dug out and refined through practice.
